title: practical english usage - Part 355
content:
We must write to him,  in order that he does/will not feel that we are I'm going to make an early start so that I don't/won't get stuck in the traffic.

I'll send the contract express so that she gets / she'll get it before Tuesday.

Present tenses are sometimes used for the future.

2 present      tenses     for   future I've come early so I can talk to you.

In an informal style, that can be dropped after so (►  265).

We send monthly reports in order that they may have full information.

I'm putting it in the oven now so that it'll be ready by seven o'clock.

vocabulary-' •  592  sort of, kind ofand type of That's a funny sort of (a) car.  What sort of (a) bird is that?

but structures with articles are possible in an informal style.

The  article a/an is usually dropped after sort of, kind of and type of, 1 articles 592 sort     of,   kind     of   and   type     of I hope you can do the repair soon - I need the car.

- He did the repair quickly but not very well.

I ought to see the doctor soon, (not an emergency) - Let's get her to the doctor quickly. (It's urgent - we  need to move fast.) Quickly refers to the speed with which  something is done. Compare: 3  quickly My watch is five minutes fast.

A  watch or clock is fast or slow, not early or late.

Best wishes for an early recovery.

I should be grateful for an early reply.

meaning as soon.

In a formal style, the adjective early can sometimes have the same kind of It's the earliest known example of a cave painting.

I caught an early train. You're very early.

Early can also be used as an adjective.

The plane arrived twenty minutes early.

Sometimes early means 'before the expected time'.

I usually get up early and go to bed early, (     L usually get up soon-. ..) not We usually take a long break early in the year, (     ... seen in the year.) Early that week, Luke was called to the police station.

thinking about'. It does not usually mean ‘ a short time after now/then'.

The  adverb early means ‘ near the beginning of the time-period that we  are 2 early For no sooner... than, ►     480.

*1'11 pay you soon.' 'The sooner the better.' The work was hard at the beginning, but she soon got used to it.

Get well soon. (Nor GetweU-earfy?) Soon means ‘ a short time after now' or ‘ a short time after then’ .

591  soon,      early      and   quickly For sometimes and once, ►    542 Sometimes, in the long winter evenings, I just sit and think about life.

I sometimes went skiing when I lived in Germany.

occasions', ‘ more than once' (past, present or future).

Sometimes (/'sʌmtaɪmz/) is an adverb of frequency (►   198.1). It means ‘ on some Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary'  •  593  sound or the other is preferred, but they are usually both possible.

There is little difference between speak and talk. In certain situations one 1 little    difference 594 speak       and   talk and                  etc    ►   442 south         southern, It sounds as if/though he's had a hard day.

That sounds like James coming up the stairs.

Sound is often followed by like or as if!though.

The car sounds / is sounding a bit rough these days.

However, progressive forms are possible when there is an idea of change.

Your idea sounds great, (     Your idea's sounding great.) Progressive forms are not very common.

You sound unhappy. What's the matter?

Sound is a linking verb (►   11). It is followed by adjectives, not adverbs.

She's kind of strange.  I've changed my mind, kind of.

I've had sort of an idea about what we could do.

Sometimes I sort of wonder whether I shouldn't sort of get a job.

We sort of thought you might forget.

exactly, or to make what we  say less definite.

expression, or at the end of a sentence, to show that we  are not speaking very In an informal style, sort of and kind of can be used before almost any word or 3 softeners Cars of that type are...

This sort of car is... These kinds of ear(s) are ...

that sort!kind!type.

above), by using plural sorts!kinds!types, or by using the structure . .. of this!

avoided in a formal style. This can be done by using a singular noun (see This structure (these sort of, etc) is often felt to be incorrect, and is usually Do you smoke those kind of cigarettes?
